title: Ethics and Professional Practice in Game Design: A South African Perspective
summary: This paper examines ethics and professional practice in game design from a South African perspective, arguing that game designers hold unique moral responsibilities given the medium's capacity to shape player behaviour, emotional states, and cognitive patterns. Eight ethical domains are surveyed: monetisation ethics and dark patterns such as loot boxes and fake currency systems; representation and cultural sensitivity with urgency in the South African colonial context; accessibility and inclusion drawing on The Last of Us Part II and Celeste; risks of AI-generated content in relation to copyright and IP theft; POPIA compliance and data protection; crunch culture and exploitative workplace practices; and environmental sustainability. The second part applies these frameworks personally, outlining integration of ethical practices throughout the design process — from ethical impact assessments in pre-production to fair monetisation models inspired by Stardew Valley and Path of Exile, accessibility in early prototyping, and authentically African narratives through diverse hiring and community consultation.
